observations.text: Seed states age 28, female, never married, city Madison, WI 53717. Interview confirms age 28 (Q85: born July 14, 1997), female, never married (Q84), born and raised in Madison (Q87), living in 53717 zip code area (Q08).
observations.source_a_ref: seed: age, sex, marital_status, city, state, zipcode

observations.text: Seed states education_level 'high_school'. Interview confirms high school diploma from East High, class of 2015 (Q90), no college enrollment (Q91, Q92).
Seed states occupation 'fast_food_or_counter_worker'. Interview confirms fast food crew member at a chain restaurant (Q16, Q65).

Seed describes cultural background as 'second-generation Italian-American raised in Madison, Wisconsin' with grandparents from Italy. Interview confirms: dad's side is Italian, grandparents came from a town south of Naples (Q01), identifies as White / Italian-American (Q89), nonna made pasta from scratch (Q01 follow-up).

Seed mentions a brother in the family context indirectly (cultural_background references 'family'). Interview reveals brother Marco, age 25, in Milwaukee doing HVAC (Q05). This adds specificity not present in the seed but does not contradict it.
Seed describes Mary as living in a Catholic, Midwestern upbringing. Interview confirms Catholic affiliation through St. Maria Goretti (Q47, Q94) and Midwestern working-class household (Q01, Q98).

Interview Q85 gives date of birth as July 14, 1997. Extracted profile records age as 28 but does not include the specific DOB. This is a minor gap -- the DOB could be useful for downstream persona modeling.
Interview Q05 details brother Marco (25, HVAC tech, Milwaukee, weekly texts). Extracted profile captures this in social_and_relationships details. Consistent.
Interview Q87 confirms born at St. Mary's Hospital in Madison. Profile states 'Madison, WI 53717' as location. Birth hospital not captured in profile, which is a very minor omission.

Demographer summary estimates annual income as approximately $23,000-$25,000 gross. Interview Q72 states take-home ~$1,900/month after taxes, with gross ~$2,200-$2,300/month. Gross annual would be ~$26,400-$27,600. The summary underestimates slightly.
Interview Q101 confirms mother has associate's degree in accounting. Demographer summary correctly notes 'mother has an associate's.' Consistent.

The demographer summary states 'annual household income of approximately $23,000-$25,000 gross,' but work_and_career details state take-home pay of ~$1,900/month. $1,900 x 12 = $22,800 take-home; gross at $2,200-$2,300/month = $26,400-$27,600. The gross income estimate in the demographer summary is slightly low relative to the work_and_career details.

Profile lists education as 'High school diploma (East High, 2015)' and occupation as 'Fast food crew member (de facto shift lead), 3 years at current location.' These are internally consistent -- no degree required for the role, and tenure matches Q01's mention of ~10 years total in food service.

Profile location states 'Madison, WI 53717' and home_and_lifestyle says 'west side Madison (53717).' Social_and_relationships mentions parents on the east side. These are geographically consistent -- Mary grew up on the east side and now lives on the west side.
Interview Q85 provides specific date of birth (July 14, 1997). This is not recorded anywhere in the extracted profile beyond the age field.
Interview Q86 confirms born in the U.S., Q87 specifies Madison, Wisconsin at St. Mary's Hospital. Place of birth not captured in profile.

Interview Q88 confirms not Hispanic/Latinx, Q89 identifies as White / Italian-American. Race/ethnicity not captured as a structured field in extracted_profile (mentioned in demographer summary but not as a top-level field).
Interview Q93 confirms no military service. This is present implicitly (not mentioned) but not captured as a structured field.

Seed mentions 'razor-sharp cash handling' and 'habit of double-checking every receipt for hidden costs.' Interview confirms: Q02 follow-up describes checking every receipt and cutting coupons during family financial strain; Q56 follow-up confirms tracking every purchase in bullet journal; Q17 follow-up details color-coded financial tracking.
observations.source_b_ref: Q02 follow-up, Q56 follow-up, Q17 follow-up

observations.text: Seed mentions 'eyeing a promotion to shift supervisor' and career goal to 'advance from crew member to shift supervisor within the next year.' Interview extensively confirms this aspiration with detailed behavioral evidence: tracking contributions in journal (Q16 follow-up), planning for Madison College enrollment (Q01 follow-up, Q18, Q91), ServSafe certification goal (Q01 follow-up).
observations.source_b_ref: Q01 follow-up, Q16 follow-up, Q18, Q91, Q108

observations.text: Seed mentions 'saving for a future diner venture' in travel_persona. Interview confirms a diner fund at $1,200 (Q61), detailed vision for the diner (Q01 follow-up, Q108), and estimated $50K-$100K needed (Q61 follow-up).
observations.source_b_ref: Q01 follow-up, Q61, Q61 follow-up, Q108

observations.text: Seed mentions 'occasional craft-brew indulgence' in persona. Interview confirms craft brewery tastings with Jess, 2-3 drinks per week maximum, tracked in journal (Q38, Q38 follow-up). Spending is carefully budgeted.

Seed mentions 'meticulously planned weekend getaways' to Door County and Napa Valley. Interview reveals very limited actual travel due to severe budget constraints (Q10 follow-up: 'everything I care about is here'; Q56-Q64 detail tight finances). The seed's travel aspirations appear overstated relative to the interview's financial reality.

Interview Q40 details 'oh no' fund at ~$600, diner fund at ~$1,200, automatic savings transfer from direct deposit (Q73). Profile work_and_career section captures diner fund at $1,200 but the emergency fund amount and automatic transfer are only partially captured. The behavioral economist summary accurately describes these dynamics.

Interview Q63 describes $800 credit card debt from brake repair, paying $50/month, goal to pay off by August. Profile work_and_career section does not include credit card debt information. This is a significant financial detail omitted from the profile.

Interview Q80 discusses potential eligibility for SNAP/benefits and identity-based resistance to applying. Profile does not capture this in any preference or detail. The behavioral_economist summary mentions resistance to government assistance, which is good.

Interview Q57 specifies monthly take-home ~$1,900-$2,000 and total spending ~$1,700-$1,800 leaving $100-$200 for savings. Profile work_and_career captures take-home at ~$1,900 but doesn't capture the total spending figure. The tight margin is described in the behavioral_economist summary.

Interview Q82 reveals that a $400 emergency would wipe most of the emergency fund and that anything over $600 would require credit card use. This specific vulnerability is not captured in the profile details but is consistent with the behavioral_economist summary about loss aversion.

Profile travel preferences mention 'meticulously planned weekend getaways' to Door County and Napa Valley, but the behavioral_economist summary correctly notes 'deferring gratification' and 'cost-minimization behaviors.' The unresolved/ambiguous section flags the gap between seed travel aspirations and interview financial reality. This is internally noted and handled.

Profile food_and_dining mentions $250-$300/month grocery spend; home_and_lifestyle mentions $900/month rent. Work_and_career shows ~$1,900 take-home. These budget figures are internally consistent and sum to a feasible monthly budget as described in Q56-Q57.

The behavioral_economist summary says Mary 'declined the Milwaukee transfer despite higher pay, weighting social capital and local knowledge over income gains.' This aligns with the social_and_relationships and home_and_lifestyle sections showing deep local rootedness. Internally consistent.
Interview Q63 provides detailed credit card debt information ($800, brake repair, $50/month payments, August payoff target). This is not captured in the extracted_profile preferences or details.
observations.should_be_in: preferences.work_and_career or a dedicated finances section

observations.text: Interview Q75 describes using free tax filing software and receiving $300-$400 refund that goes to diner fund. The tax refund-to-savings pipeline is not captured in the profile.

Interview Q59 describes seasonal bill fluctuation (heating bill swing from $30 summer to $150+ winter, December holiday deficit). Profile mentions rent and take-home but doesn't capture seasonal financial stress patterns.

Interview Q38 follow-up: Mary tracks craft beer spending in her journal. This specific budget-tracking behavior for discretionary spending is captured in the behavioral_economist summary as 'identity-affirming expenditures she carefully budgets.'

Seed does not contain explicit political information. Interview reveals Mary as an irregular voter who participates in presidential elections, leans center-left on economic issues (Q23, Q24, Q95, Q96). No contradiction since seed is silent on politics.

Seed's cultural_background describes 'Catholic, Midwestern upbringing.' Interview confirms Catholic church attendance (Q47, Q94) but shows no faith-based political alignment. This is consistent -- the seed describes culture, not politics.

Seed mentions 'community' in cultural_background and career_goals. Interview reveals community engagement through church (Q47), running club (Q07), community garden (Q08), but limited formal civic participation. The seed's 'community' emphasis is aspirational rather than deeply civic.

Seed does not mention race or racial attitudes. Interview reveals nuanced racial awareness developed through workplace interactions with DeShawn (Q26, Q26 follow-up) and Italian-American immigrant family history (Q28). No contradiction.
observations.source_b_ref: Q26, Q26 follow-up, Q27, Q28, Q29

observations.text: Interview Q23 states Mary votes in presidential elections, misses some local ones due to work schedule, early votes when possible. Profile community_and_civic section captures this accurately: 'Votes in presidential elections; occasionally misses local elections due to work schedule; Early votes when possible.'

Interview Q24 provides detailed political views: supports minimum wage increase, affordable healthcare, personal responsibility, frustrated with polarization. The political_scientist summary captures this as 'economically motivated voter' leaning 'center-left on pocketbook issues.' Accurate.

Interview Q95 reveals Mary almost attended a minimum-wage rally organized by Jess. Profile states 'Does not attend protests, rallies, or political events.' While technically she did not attend, the near-miss reveals more ambivalence than the profile suggests.

Interview Q25 follow-up discusses fast food worker protections and tipped wages as issues Mary follows closely. Profile does not specifically mention these workplace-specific political interests, though they are implied by the political_scientist summary.
observations.source_b_ref: extracted_profile: preferences.community_and_civic (no specific policy interests listed)

observations.text: The political_scientist summary states 'Institutional trust is low regarding healthcare and workplace protections.' Interview Q36, Q66, Q76 support this -- frustration with insurance costs, no union, poor benefits. Accurate characterization.

The political_scientist summary describes 'low-engagement' voter and community_and_civic section says she 'does not attend protests, rallies, or political events.' These are consistent with each other.

The community_and_civic section lists St. Maria Goretti attendance and the political_scientist summary says 'no signs of faith-based political alignment.' These are consistent -- religious practice and political behavior are decoupled in the profile.
Interview Q25 follow-up mentions tipped wages and fast food worker protections as specific policy interests. These are not listed in the community_and_civic details or elsewhere in the profile.

Interview Q30 follow-up reveals dynamic between Mary and Jess on political activism -- Jess respects Mary's quieter approach. This social-political dynamic is captured in social_and_relationships and the political_scientist summary.

Interview Q42 discusses trusted information sources (NPR, CDC, nurse practitioner, pharmacy). Profile media_and_information_diet captures NPR and CDC but does not mention the nurse practitioner or pharmacy as health information sources.
Interview Q23 follow-up mentions early voting as a strategy to avoid shift conflicts. This specific civic behavior is captured in community_and_civic details.

Seed describes Mary as 'routine-obsessed, bullet-journal aficionado' with 'disciplined work ambition.' Interview provides extensive behavioral evidence: 20-minute nightly journal ritual (Q17 follow-up), 4+ years of filled journals, color-coded systems, pros-and-cons lists for decisions (Q03), rigorous daily planning. Strongly consistent.
observations.source_b_ref: Q03, Q15, Q17 follow-up, Q56 follow-up

observations.text: Seed describes a 'competitive edge.' Interview confirms competitive elements through Sudoku puzzles (Q15), running accountability (Q07 follow-up), and the pattern of tracking performance metrics at work (Q16 follow-up: 'I've got receipts').

Seed describes 'disciplined, routine-driven work ethic' and 'calmly resolve high-pressure customer issues.' Interview provides detailed behavioral evidence: staying calm with difficult customers (Q16 follow-up), rush-hour composure (Q65 follow-up), pushing through headaches at work (Q34 follow-up).
observations.source_b_ref: Q16 follow-up, Q65, Q65 follow-up, Q34 follow-up

observations.text: Seed describes 'reading practical self-help books' as a hobby. Interview confirms: Q50 follow-up mentions reading a self-help book about habits that inspired the 'one good thing' journal entry. Consistent.

Seed mentions 'attending local art walks and museum exhibits to unwind.' Interview does not provide direct evidence of art walk or museum attendance. The closest reference is Wes Anderson films (Q15 follow-up) and Instagram browsing of food accounts (Q48). This seed claim lacks interview corroboration.
observations.source_b_ref: Q15 follow-up, Q48 (no art walk/museum evidence)

observations.text: Seed mentions 'solving Sudoku puzzles for a competitive edge.' Interview Q15 mentions 'did some Sudoku' in the evening routine. The 'competitive edge' framing is from the seed, while the interview presents it as casual evening activity. Minor emphasis difference but not contradictory.

Interview Q45 describes lying awake at night running through scenarios, heart racing, inability to shut brain off -- occurring since high school, before tests, job interviews, reviews. The psychologist summary accurately captures this as 'anticipatory rumination before decisions' and uses behavioral language only, avoiding clinical labels.

Interview Q43 describes winter low-energy periods: 2-3 weeks in late January/early February with flat energy, reduced motivation, minimal journal entries. Psychologist summary describes 'seasonal mood dips in winter' and profile health_and_wellness captures 'low-energy flat periods in deep winter.' Accurate behavioral description without clinical labeling.

Interview Q45 follow-up describes self-doubt spirals ('what am I doing, I'm twenty-eight working fast food, am I ever going to get anywhere'), social comparison with high school classmates, and self-critical thinking when tired. The psychologist summary captures 'effective self-regulation' and 'narrative identity' but the specific self-doubt and social comparison patterns are underrepresented.
observations.source_b_ref: extracted_profile: summaries.psychologist (self-doubt spiral not specifically captured)

observations.text: Interview Q44 details emotional processing after breakup: went for a 9 PM run, cried while making pasta, channeled grief into cooking (froze 10 portions of red sauce). Profile social_and_relationships states 'Processes relationship stress through physical activity (running) and cooking.' Accurate.

Interview Q45 follow-up: Jess suggested therapy with sliding-scale fees; Mary acknowledges it might help but avoids it ('the idea of sitting in a room and talking about my feelings to a stranger sounds exhausting'). Psychologist summary captures this as 'mild avoidance patterns around professional help' intersecting financial constraints with identity-based resistance. Accurate.

The psychologist summary describes 'high conscientiousness' and 'moderate introversion,' while social_and_relationships shows weekly social outings (Jess), Saturday running group, 2-3x/week parent visits. The 'moderate introversion' is consistent -- she has regular social contact but prefers one-on-one or small group settings and processes emotions internally.

The psychologist summary describes 'moderate-low openness' gravitating toward 'routine, familiarity, and practical knowledge over novelty-seeking.' The entertainment preferences include indie music (Bon Iver) and Wes Anderson films, which could suggest somewhat higher openness. However, these are comfort-oriented preferences rather than novelty-seeking, so the characterization holds.

The health_and_wellness section lists 'lies awake running through scenarios before big decisions; occurs 1-2 times per month.' The sensory_and_physical_context in others_misc also lists 'Intermittent sleep disruption (1-2 times/month) tied to anticipatory rumination.' These are redundant entries of the same behavior in two different profile locations, but they are consistent with each other.

Interview Q39 follow-up describes witnessing a high school friend's descent into painkiller misuse and its formative impact on Mary's cautious attitude toward medications. This experience is not captured in the profile, though it partially explains her avoidance of prescriptions and substances.

Interview Q11 follow-up describes Mary's emotional reaction to disruption of her organized living space: 'If someone else was in here moving stuff around I'd lose it.' Profile home_and_lifestyle captures the organization (alphabetized spice rack, etc.) but not the emotional intensity of her attachment to environmental control.

Interview Q109 expresses core values: consistency, family, quiet hard work, routines as control, small pleasures, having a dream. Profile captures most of this across various sections. The psychologist summary alludes to these values through its behavioral analysis.
observations.should_be_in: distributed across summaries and preferences (adequately covered)

observations.text: Interview Q37 follow-up mentions gardening as 'kind of therapeutic' and running as a mental reset. Profile nature_and_outdoors captures 'finds gardening therapeutic and calming' and the psychologist summary mentions 'exercise, journaling, and cooking as behavioral coping mechanisms.' Adequately captured.

Interview Q46 follow-up reveals Mary's worry about her mother's emotional wellbeing and her tendency to take on caregiving and emotional labor for both parents. This caregiving dynamic is partially captured in social_and_relationships but the emotional labor component is underrepresented.

inconsistencies_found: ISSUE: Demographer summary estimates gross annual income at $23,000-$25,000, but interview Q72 indicates gross monthly of $2,200-$2,300, yielding $26,400-$27,600 annually.

resolutions.issue: Demographer summary underestimates gross annual income
resolutions.resolution: The demographer summary should be updated to read 'approximately $26,000-$28,000 gross' to match interview-derived figures. Take-home of ~$1,900/month ($22,800/year) is the net figure.

gaps_addressed.gap: Seed travel persona (Door County, Napa Valley, Kyoto) largely unsupported by interview evidence due to budget constraints
gaps_addressed.resolution: Profile correctly flags this in others_misc.unresolved_or_ambiguous. Travel preferences should be understood as aspirational rather than behavioral. Carry forward with annotation.

gaps_addressed.gap: Seed arts_persona mentions 'attending local art walks and museum exhibits' but no interview evidence confirms this behavior
gaps_addressed.resolution: Profile correctly flags this in others_misc.unresolved_or_ambiguous. The arts_and_culture preference section includes it with the note '(arts persona from seed data).' Carry forward as seed-originated but unverified.

gaps_addressed.gap: Credit card debt ($800), tax refund to diner fund, and seasonal bill fluctuation not captured in extracted_profile details
gaps_addressed.resolution: These financial details from Q63, Q75, Q59 should be added to a finances subsection or to work_and_career details for completeness.

gaps_addressed.gap: DOB, birthplace, race/ethnicity not captured as structured top-level fields in extracted_profile
gaps_addressed.resolution: These demographic details from Q85, Q87, Q88, Q89 are available in the interview but were not extracted as structured fields. Adding them would improve downstream usability.

gaps_addressed.gap: High school friend's substance misuse experience (Q39 follow-up) not captured as formative event
gaps_addressed.resolution: This explains Mary's cautious attitude toward medications and is relevant to her health behavior profile. Minor enrichment opportunity.

expert_summaries.demographer: Mary Alberti is a 28-year-old never-married white female of second-generation Italian-American descent, born and raised in Madison, Wisconsin. She holds a high school diploma and has worked in food service for approximately ten years, currently earning roughly $26,000-$28,000 gross annually as a fast food crew member in the 53717 zip code. Her family's socioeconomic trajectory -- father as a disabled machinist, mother as a part-time bookkeeper, both with high-school-level education -- situates her firmly in the Midwestern working class. She is at a demographic inflection point, pursuing credentialing through Madison College's hospitality program that could alter her occupational trajectory, while her strong attachment to Madison and her family constrains geographic mobility.
expert_summaries.behavioral_economist: Mary demonstrates sophisticated present-bias mitigation through her bullet-journal commitment device, which tracks every expenditure and organizes savings goals across emergency and diner-fund accounts (Q17, Q56, Q61). She exhibits strong loss aversion around her $600 emergency fund and $1,200 diner fund, showing visible distress at depletion events from brake repairs and lost sick-day wages (Q62, Q63, Q64). Her consumption decisions reflect future-orientation: she consistently defers gratification by cutting social spending, eating cheaply, and picking up extra shifts to maintain savings targets (Q57, Q60, Q81). Her identity utility is heavily tied to self-reliance, creating a revealed preference gap where she forgoes likely-eligible government benefits due to identity cost (Q80). Risk aversion is modulated by social capital -- she declined a Milwaukee transfer with higher pay because local knowledge, relationships, and the diner dream were place-dependent (Q02).
expert_summaries.political_scientist: Mary is a low-engagement, economically driven voter who participates in presidential elections and occasionally in local ones, using early voting to navigate irregular work schedules (Q23). She gravitates toward candidates advocating minimum wage increases, affordable healthcare, and worker protections, positioning her as center-left on pocketbook issues without partisan self-identification (Q24, Q95, Q96). Her civic footprint is small: she attends a Catholic church, participates in a community garden and running club, but avoids rallies, protests, and public political expression (Q29, Q47). Her racial awareness evolved through direct workplace exposure to a Black coworker's experiences with policing (Q26), producing an internal attitudinal shift rather than external activism. Institutional trust is low regarding healthcare and employment protections, shaped by direct experience with inadequate benefits and unpaid sick leave (Q36, Q66, Q76).
expert_summaries.psychologist: Mary shows high conscientiousness through her pervasive planning behavior -- a four-year bullet-journal practice with color-coded daily, weekly, and monthly systems for tracking work, finances, fitness, and meals (Q17 follow-up). She displays moderate introversion, preferring one-on-one connections with Jess and her mother over group social settings, and processes emotions internally before sharing them (Q29, Q43 follow-up, Q49). Anticipatory rumination before major decisions -- lying awake with a racing heart, running through every scenario -- has been a stable behavioral pattern since high school (Q45), occurring approximately twice monthly. She experiences seasonal energy and motivation dips in deep winter that resolve with warmer weather and outdoor activity resumption (Q43). Her primary behavioral coping mechanisms are physical activity (running), structured productivity (journaling, cooking, meal prep), and social support from a single strong-tie friend (Q37 follow-up, Q44, Q50). She exhibits mild avoidance of professional support services, attributing this to both financial barriers and a self-reliance orientation transmitted through family values (Q45 follow-up, Q80).

additions: Add credit card debt details ($800, $50/month payments, August payoff target) to work_and_career or a finances subsection
Add tax refund (~$300-$400) to diner fund pipeline to work_and_career details
Add seasonal financial stress pattern (winter heating spikes from ~$30 to $150+, December holiday deficits) to home_and_lifestyle
Add specific policy interests (tipped wages, fast food worker protections) to community_and_civic
Add DOB (July 14, 1997), birthplace (Madison, WI), and race/ethnicity (White, Italian-American, not Hispanic) as structured top-level fields
Add formative experience of witnessing high school friend's substance misuse to life_events_and_transitions
Add emotional intensity of environmental control needs (distress at disruption of organized space) to psychologist summary or home_and_lifestyle
corrections: Update demographer summary income estimate from '$23,000-$25,000 gross' to '$26,000-$28,000 gross'

final_status: Mary Alberti's interview and extracted profile are strongly consistent with the NVIDIA seed data across all four expert domains. One minor inconsistency was found (demographer income estimate slightly low). Several completeness gaps were identified -- primarily missing financial details (credit card debt, seasonal bill patterns, tax refund pipeline) and missing structured demographic fields (DOB, birthplace, race). Seed-originated claims about travel behavior and art walk attendance remain unverified by interview evidence but are appropriately flagged in the profile's unresolved section. The psychologist domain uses behavioral language throughout without clinical labels. Overall, the profile faithfully represents the interview data with high accuracy and no major contradictions. Verdict: PASS_WITH_REVISIONS.
